summary: Gruber announces Markdown 1.0b4, a bug-fix release with improved Blosxom integration. He highlights Aaron Swartz's html2text Python script, which converts HTML back into Markdown-formatted plain text, noting he plans to use it to convert his old Daring Fireball articles written in raw HTML. He acknowledges that perfect round-trip fidelity between Markdown and HTML isn't possible but praises html2text for coming close. He also mentions Gust's HumaneText, which makes Markdown available through Mac OS X's Services menu.
key_points: Markdown 1.0b4 is a bug-fix release with improved Blosxom integration, recommended for all earlier beta users
Aaron Swartz's html2text enables reverse translation from HTML to Markdown-formatted plain text, filling a gap users had been asking about
Complete round-trip fidelity between Markdown and HTML is acknowledged as impossible, but html2text comes close enough for practical use
The Markdown ecosystem is growing with third-party tools like HumaneText bringing it to Mac OS X Services and editors like SubEthaEdit and TextEdit
money_quotes: A handful of people have asked if there's a way to translate Markdown in reverse — to turn HTML back into Markdown-formatted plain text.
html2text is a Python script that convers a page of HTML into clean, easy-to-read plain ASCII. Better yet, that ASCII also happens to be valid Markdown.
html2text works so well that I'm planning to use it to convert most of my old Daring Fireball articles (the ones I wrote in raw HTML).
That sort of complete round-trip fidelity simply is not possible, but html2text comes pretty close.
Markdown and html2text are now available from the Mac OS X Services menu, thanks to Gust's HumaneText.
key_insight: The Markdown ecosystem was rapidly maturing beyond Gruber's original tool, with third-party developers building complementary utilities like HTML-to-Markdown conversion and OS-level integration that expanded its reach and utility.
